So in 2004, as most of you know, not all of you, but most of you have been around for a while, I know that I took over running Kennedy Inner Circle and became Glaser Kennedy Inner Circle and then we immediately offered a level of coaching which is Gold Plus Telecoaching. How many of you in the room are members of Gold Plus? Yeah, that's what I would have thought. So if you turn to page 180, you will actually see the sales letter in there that provides the Gold Plus. Oh, I said 180, but that must be a mistake. 178. No. I'm sorry, it's not. 186. Let's try that. It helps when I put my glasses on. 186. It's called a letter of regret. And this is a telecoaching program. We've been providing it since 2004. We now have about 460 members in Gold Plus Telecoaching. The investment for that is $240 a month. And for that, there's a whole list of benefits that in the letter that people get, but they get a one-on-one, they get a group call with Dan and I each month. Either it's just Dan and I or some, and most months we have a guest expert on the phone with us. At the end of the call, we also open it up for Q&A. We send out a CD of the call afterwards, which is critical on a telecoaching program to send out a CD of the call. You must do that. If not, you're going to have a lot of dropout. We also add with that a gathering once a year where Gold Plus members get to come together. Initially it was designed in a way where Gold Plus members get to come together. And Dan and I put on a small program, and you got to ask questions of Dan and I. What we actually found out after a very, just by observation, we discovered the fact that what Gold Plus members really want to do is get to know other Gold Plus members. So we added a couple additional programs to that throughout the year where our gatherings, people get to get up and introduce themselves and then people can go seek out each other and work out how they can network with each other. So that was a very important lesson that we learned. Also a lesson I think for many of you to think about is a lot of the people that are in your programs, they want to be able to rub elbows with each other as much as they even get to know you. And then we give them other benefits such as Gold Plus inserts in your newsletter that you know about. We have some special times that people can call in for the Gold Plus membership in order to ask questions of Dan and I throughout the year. And they also get the largest discount on any of our events. So those of you that are in this room here, you've got a larger discount by virtue of the fact that you're a Gold Plus member. In most cases, people that attend our events, the membership almost pays for itself by just the savings that you get by attending events. So that's how that program is kind of structured. We also, also in Glaser, Kenny and A Circle, I do some of that one-on-one two-hour thing for the $2,000 fee. I mean, the problem with that with me now is it's about a six to nine month wait before Sherry can find an hour on my schedule in order to put those in. So people are often asked to wait. We get some amusing ones like last week somebody called up the office and said, I'm a Gold member and I'd like to buy 15 minutes of Bill's time. How much does that cost? So Sherry called him back and says, you can't buy 15 minutes of Bill's time, which is another important lesson is you've got to structure what you're willing to provide. In my particular case, I used to sell an hour of telecoaching and I found out that one-on-one telecoaching, I actually found out that the two hours is a much better way to go. First of all, it's $2,000, opposed to $1,000. And secondly, it's a much better call because I'm preparing the client before they get on the phone and I'm prepared before I get on the phone. So that's a way to structure those. We also have on page 174, we have our two international now mastermind programs. We have Info Mastermind. Who here in the room is an Info Mastermind member? So I got five of you in the room, Info Mastermind. And then we have our VIP program. Who was in VIP? I got three, four, five, six in the VIP in the room. So these are programs where we get together three times a year in a true mastermind session. And we basically, the basic format is as long as I can keep everybody, I can ride her and everybody. The basic format is everybody gets a turn in the room to go around to somewhere between minimal 30 minutes, usually closer to 45 minutes, occasionally an hour if it's a really big issue where everybody goes around the room, talks about what's happened in their business since the last time we met. What's working in their business? That's the second thing they talk about. And the third thing is what do they want to pose to the group in terms of challenges they have or questions they have or where they need help. The last part is where we spend the largest amount of our time. But as you can imagine when we talk about what's working in your business, everybody's ears perk up because they want to figure out what's working in your business so I can take it into my business. And big breakthroughs happen here and looking around the hands that went up, you know, I know all of you or just about all of you had big, I don't want to mention what they are because you may not want me to disclose some of your big breakthroughs. But mostly everybody has close to a doubling of their business within a one year period of time. So really very, very valuable types of groups. And as you can see there is I showed you all the different benefits of that's included in there. So for those of you that want to figure out how to structure a group like this, you can see the kind of benefits that we provide to this. I mean in our particular case last year we had about 100 applicants that we turned away big demand for groups of this size. And then the next thing is because we had such a large demand on page 178 we, you'll see this is our newest program and this is the one that we're doing with, I'm doing to collectively with Lee Miltier who Lee is back there in the room in the teal suit. That she is, we actually joined forces on this group whereas we're now providing two different types of structures. One structure is Lee is a performance coach. So so many people are constantly saying to us all the time, you know, my biggest problem is I don't have enough time, how do I get things done, how do I maximize my performance. Well that's Lee's area of expertise so she's spending half of the time doing that and then I'm spending the other half of the time tackling marketing issues. So that's a group that we're doing together and there's about 75 people that are in that group and that's a group that we still have a few openings for if anybody has any interest in what you have, you actually have the sales letter right here in your manual so if you have any interest you can seek out Lee on one of the breaks or Sherry and they'd be more than happy to tell you a bit more about that. So that's what my coaching and consulting business looks like generating somewhere around $3 million a year in business and coaching and consulting. So I've done this for some time now and you can see how I've done it. So I want to talk to you about a couple of the big things I've learned, 10 big lessons I learned. First of all the vast majority of clients screw things up. That's a real big lesson that I've learned. And you've got to sort of understand that. And the reason why I tell you that is not because I don't like clients or members or what not, it's just the fact that you can't allow that to affect you. That's a real big thing, it's because you have a real vision in your head of how things are supposed to work or how they're supposed to go back and do things. And in most cases they don't do it the way that you had a vision for them to do it. And so you just got to get past it because if you can't get past it then you're going to hate doing coaching and consulting. So if you take it real personal it's just not going to work out for you. I'm going to talk about the clients, I'm talking about it for the time being. The truth of the matter is, and you'll find out in just a few minutes, it doesn't really matter that the vast majority of them screw it up. First of all if the vast majority of them do something they're going to get a better result than they had had they not done something. And then actually the other thing you'll find out in just a few moments is there's various reasons why people are in groups or become clients. The second thing is you must get over the attitude that you're responsible for their results which kind of links to number one and number two. They kind of link together. So because they screw it up you just can't feel responsible for their results. And then this is number three which is what I talked about before which is the value of the client comes from three main sources. Number one is the knowledge that they receive. And it happens a lot, they come into the groups and they pick up one idea and they take it back and they apply that one idea to their business and it has a huge growth to their business. So the value and a lot of people in the groups because they're there for value. Another reason why they're in the groups is the ability to show off. And this is a big thing, is the ability to show off. And you can't, not only can you not forget this but if you don't allow it you're going to have people being unhappy in the groups. They have to have, some of them, not all of them have to have the ability to show off. I've said this before, I've said it many times, being an entrepreneur is one of the loneliest jobs in the world because it's real hard to really share with other people what's going on in your business. I mean if you, with your employees, if you're showing off how well your business is doing, then the first thing that happens is that they want to have a raise. That's like a terrible thing. So they have really no place to show off. And so a lot of people really need that. And those of you that are in here that are in one of my groups, you know this is true. We got people and they know every one of those meetings that are there just because they want a spot to show off what's happening and that's good. There's nothing wrong with that. And then the third thing is they need a place to belong which again goes back to this whole thing as people are lonely. And where the hell are they going to talk to anybody about what's going on in their business? So you can't confuse the two. Showing off is different from place to belong. Some people don't need a place to belong but they need a place to show off and some people don't need a place to show off but they need a place to belong. Some people need both. And so a lot of people the best ones are doing all three and there's nothing wrong with that at all. But that's the reason why, that's the three main reasons why clients join your groups or become clients of yours. Number four is you can't forget to constantly cover the basics. And that's a real big thing about being the coaching and consulting business is that what happens is that when you're working with clients either in a large group format or one on one what happens is you assume that because they're in this relationship with you either by this group or one on one that they understand everything that happened to get them there. And a lot of times they don't or they forget it or the basics are some of the things that generate some of the biggest incomes to them. So you can never forget to go back and to cover those basics. Number something. Number five. Number five is a lot of the value that I bring is the ability to ask questions and challenge different premises that they have. And these are actually two different things. Like this morning Paul, where's Paul? Paul, I had breakfast with Paul. Paul's in my VIP group this year. He's new to the VIP group this year. He hasn't yet had his first meeting. And he said to me, yes, Bill, you mind, I have a couple of questions to ask you. You mind having breakfast with me. So I had breakfast with him. And he asked me a specific question about a situation that's going on in his advice of what I had for him about something that's going on in his business. And I don't know if he even realized it or not, but I didn't answer his question. I asked him a question back which answered his question for him. And so, I mean, basically, my question was, well, you know, he had an either or decision and I said to him, what's your reasons for you wanting to do this? Will it be the reasons for you wanting to do this? And I kind of asked him the question. He answered his own question, but that's one of the biggest things that you bring to the table is the ability to ask questions. And then the other thing is the ability to challenge premises. And a lot of times with most entrepreneurs, I have found this on my entire life is that most times when people are doing dumb things, if you ask them why they're doing dumb things in their business, the most common answer is that's the way we've always done it. And you got to get people to get past that. And a lot of people just keep doing things over and over again because that's the way we've always done it. And when I think back in my own retail menswear business, we had a lot of those things we always did because we always did it. And it was, you know, we had a constant, I needed oftentimes fresh eyes to come into my business and to say to me, hey, you don't need to be doing that, you know, and those are big things that a coach or consultant brings to a business. Number six is, and Dan kind of mentioned it, you actually, Dan mentioned it yesterday, very more emphatically than I thought he would have mentioned it, but it was the best way to get clients to through speaking. And whenever you speak, you can always get clients if you want to get them. Now, Dan and I are sort of in the client repel mode, we try to repel as many clients as possible right now. But it's, but if I wanted to, whenever I go and speak, I could just pick up another half a dozen clients anytime because they swarm you at the end, they got questions for you. And if I wanted to that, if I wanted to, I could structure my answers to my questions to lead to a client relationship if I wanted to. Now, most cases, I don't want to do that. But if, but if you want to do that, if you're looking for clients, speaking is the best way to go out and get it because you are positioned as the authority up there and they just come and they just gravitate to you because you're in front of the room speaking, it's your position as somebody that knows something that they want to know. They want to know that information. Number seven is the more you charge, the more value your advice, the more you charge, the more they value your advice and implement. And this was, gee, that's like really good news for everybody who's in the coaching consulting business. And you just got to remember that. And, and anytime, and I'm, I'm kind of guilty. I'm more, I'm, I wish I was like a dance mode on this. I can't get to do what he does. But I'm like, I'm still like guilty. Like when I out with a friend of mine who I care a lot about and I see something really stupid, I like mention it to him. And that's like, and I tell them like how to fix it. And that's like really bad thing to do. It's first of all, it's like free advice and they value it as free advice. And it means nothing to them. And the, and it's still hard for me to get past that. And however, it's, it's not good for them. It really is the kind of situation that the more I would charge them, if all of a sudden I change it right now to a relationship where, where I'm charging them $1,000 for this piece of information, then much, much better chance that they'll listen to it and implement it. So you got to be actually not only is it good news for you because you'll be making more money, but it's good news for the relationship. Because if you want them to value the relationship and implement the relationship more, you need to really charge more for it. Number eight, another big lesson I learned is it can lead to as much work as you wanted to. So again, if you're doing the coaching consulting business, I mean, every time I do a, I mean, my time right now is just very, very limited. But if I wanted to do a lot of copywriting work, every time I am in a VIP meeting or an info mastermind meeting, people come up to me all the time and say, Bill, I need this sales letter. What's your time frame? What do you know? What do you got available? I know Dan, you know, same thing happens to Dan every time he's in one of his meetings. They're, they're swarming him, you know, LeGrand is, is all over it. Dan, I need 18 sales letters for 18 different niches. You know, I don't care how much money it is, you know. So, so it can lead to as much work as you wanted to lead to, not only additional copywriting work, in my case, copywriting, but any kind of work that you wanted to lead to. It can lead to speaking if you wanted to be, to be speaking. It can lead to additional coaching and or one-on-one consulting. Oftentimes, people in my VIP group or info mastermind groups, they come up to me and they want to buy an additional day of coaching. In some cases, I am now making that available to them. As a matter of fact, I'm giving them priority in times that they can actually see me for, for that. So, so it can lead to as much additional work as you wanted to lead to. Number nine. Number nine is you need to manage the length of the arrangement. And this is kind of a biggie, which I don't know if I heard Dan mention it yesterday or not. I wasn't in here the entire time. I did have to go to the bathroom two or three times yesterday. So, I might have missed a few things he said. But, but regardless of the relation, even if you want the relationship to go on forever, you need to structure in a way where if you want to exit that relationship, you can still exit that relationship. So, even if you're selling a program that just keeps on going on and on and on and on and on and on, you still want to manage it in a way where you have an exit if you wanted to exit it. Because let's say it's large group mastermind programs. We have 20 in the group and you're charging whatever $1,000 a month and it just keeps on going on and on until they quit. You may not want that person in your group indefinitely. Something might happen to that person that no longer makes for that person being still good into your group. Certainly, if you're on a client retainer relationship, and I have, as I said, I got three clients on retainer right now that pay me $5,000 a month. Even with those people that every month we get a check from, $5,000, $5,000, they know that I have the ability to exit this thing anytime that I want to, even the parameters that I set up with them in advance, which is, in their particular case, is 90 days notice. So, you want to always have a relationship where you have the ability that you can end it if you want to. And number 10 is, the other big lesson I learned is authority. And authority is absolutely critical. And Dan mentioned it yesterday with you. I don't know that he used the word authority, but he certainly mentioned it in a way that is the exact same point that I am making to you is that they got to always sort of understand that you are in control of this relationship. They're not in control of the relationship. And you have to be in, you have to have that authority over them. And if you don't have it, then the whole thing is going to go amuck. Particularly, by the way, in the mastermind programs, those kind of programs, because I know a lot of situations, by the way, where people have lost the authority in their mastermind programs. And then what happens invariably is people start splintering off into their own little mastermind groups. And that's not a good thing to happen when you're in those kind of relationships. So, you have to constantly have that authority in your groups when you are doing those types of things. So, those are the 10 big things that I've learned. And I'm more than happy if anybody has any questions, specifically about structure. It was one of the things we didn't talk about as structure. So, I'm just starting a number of levels of coaching programs modeled very much on what you guys are doing. And I'd like to be able to make some projections about how many people will be in each program, at each level, based on how many come into the funnel. Any idea on how to do that? No, because I wish I had a better answer for you, but a lot of it depends on prior relationship. So, as I said to you, maybe I didn't make it as clear. At Glaser Kennedy Inner Circle, it's actually easier for us to sell coaching. And we have to provide less in terms of guarantees because of strong prior relationship. Whereas in my BGS marketing system, since I sort of semi-neglect those folks, I don't have the same relationship. So, I have to work harder to get them into coaching and I have to give them more of a guarantee. Now, in your particular case, only because I know a little bit about your business from the three and a half minutes we discussed in the back of the room this morning, which is more to find out Dave Dee was doing a good job or not. That was really my intent. But you don't have a real long prior history with these folks. So, in your particular case, I would consider this idea of giving some kind of a strong guarantee. And with that, that will make the sale much, much easier. The other thing I would tell you is this. If you're different levels of coaching, I would clearly only start with, I would start with a maximum of two. I would not give people more than two decisions to make. Okay? And then you also have to think about, the other things you're going to have to think about is what's in A and what's in B. And that needs to have a lot of thought that goes into that as well because you probably want to make B as attractive as you possibly can, B and B that's the better one or the higher priced one. So you have to figure out what's in that and what's in A. And then the other thing you've got to be thinking about is how do I send people from A to B? Okay? And so there's a lot of that thought that has to go into your program as well. You know, just to sort of peel back the curtain for us a little bit. Because there's such a huge weight for us to get into one of our national coaching programs, VIP, InfoMastermind, Platinum. We actually are giving priority to people who are in peak performers to now get into that program. So you might be, so that's a way we have structured that program to make it kind of, you know, enticing the people. So that maybe I can get eventually, if I want to, to move up to another level of program. So there's a lot of decisions that need to go into that. And I hope that some of this information gives you some help with that. But there's no way I can give you actual percentages without knowing the prior relationship. Can you share some numbers for you guys in terms of how many you have at each level? Or is that too secretive? Well, we have, as I said, we have about 465 people, Gold Plus. We have 75 people, peak performers. I have 20 people in VIP, 22 people in InfoMastermind. And Dan has, he showed you yesterday, it's in your manual, the two different levels that he has in Platinum. He has group, I think it's called group one and group two. And I think combined, it's about 40 people give or take in those levels. And how many are coming into the funnel at the free 798 or whatever to get there? Very few. Most of, most of, almost everybody, not all, not everybody, but almost everybody who will send through Gold Plus. So Gold Plus is usually the first place, not all, but mostly people. Now there are some people that will like, you know, scoop right in. I mean, we have IBAs, our independent business advisors. We had two of them that purchased an area that were still in the three month free trial that invested in an area. So you do have some people that are just, you know, skyrocket right to the top. I mean, I had one gentleman yesterday, he works with Dr. Arthur Donnell in the chiropractic business. He was all over me in the back of the room. Bill, I want to join everything you got. You know what I mean? So, I mean, you got those people that they want to move real fast and they want to want as much as you as they can get. But most people, there needs to be some kind of an ascension model. And the way you structure that ascension model, a lot of thought has to be given to that because you want to always sort of let people sort of reveal to them there's something next waiting for them. You are. I've got a business called We Buy Houses. It operates in every country outside the United States. And business is getting real big for us in the UK, except for me to fly to the UK all the time is getting really hard on my time. So what I've decided I've got to do is start selling it off like in having a few representatives over there who charge say $30,000, $40,000 or $50,000 a year to be a part of the whole show. So my question is to get that sort of money, I get that sort of money in Australia, but people have not been around there for years, but I've got to go in the UK and just knock these people over. The question is to do that, do I have to door it off the back of a one day event or a two day event? Because I've done the stage thing in London and they buy a package of $2,000 or $3,000 of your package, but heck it's a really slow way to then keep flying back over there to get a few people at the big dollars. I want to get a few people in the UK at the big dollars and then make them license out under them. Is the best way to capture those people is to be doing it off the back of a one day event? Do you see what I'm saying? Yeah. Well I mean it's not, it's a good way, it's not the only way, but I mean it's certainly going over there and doing an event over there and selling them into this program is a good way. The, if you do it, the one piece of advice that I have for you about that is, it's got to be sort of like the secret discovered in Australia is now the hellable to the people in the UK because everybody sort of likes to buy the secret. You know, when I went over to Australia and spoke over there, it was like a big thing that people from the United States were coming over there. We had four speakers that were coming over there, so it's got to be a big thing that people are coming from the, you know, this is something that you got to really be building on how successful this is in Australia and now this is now available to the UK. The other model that probably would work as well and may be less painful to you considering the fact that it sounds like you don't want to do the speaking is to develop really a UK partner that now you can build the success around his story. So find somebody over, for lack of a better terminology, find somebody over in the UK that sort of becomes like your super franchise for the UK. And now, you know, and now he's the person that's responsible to be providing all these area franchises over there or area licenses or however you decide to structure that. And now you sort of build the story on his back where now he's discovered you and it'd be best if he actually does it himself and he has great success to share. So you're sort of looking for a business partner over there but also has a great story to tell on his success by using what he's learned from you and applying it to the UK market. And now let him do all the grunt work of going out and selling them as well. So if I do that, and that's one of the things I've had to discuss, what percentage of the action do I give him because I've got to go out in the UK, how much of the dollars do I give him and how much do I give? Well, that's a discussion. I mean, that's an impossible answer for me to give you without knowing all the economics and finances of your business. But my short answer is as little as you have to. But I don't know how much, you know, what's there, you know, so I don't know how much money is there. Okay. I have two questions. One of them is about authority, the whole issue of authority. You mentioned that people may go often splinter off once they get to know each other. How have you dealt with that or have you had to deal with that because it's a legitimate issue? Well, I never let it get to that point. I mean, you have to, when you're running it, when you're in a group meeting, a mastermind setting, you have to still make sure that you're in total control of that setting. And you just can never let it get to the point where you are no longer in control of that setting. So for example, you have to have a very definite, this is what's going to go on here. Here's the program that's happening here and we're sticking to the program. And because of that, because you have a program and you've developed the program, that in and of itself is a strong reason for people to be fearful of leaving you anyway. Because, you know, you're the one that developed the program and now, you know, every time, you know, Bill comes or every time Marlene comes, she's got something that she's bringing that only Marlene has. Okay? So, you know, if you're answering the question specifically to your mastermind groups in your IBA area, you know, I mean, you have two things. I mean, A is you've got the Dan Kennedy story and the Dan Kennedy DVDs and the Dan Kennedy program for that day. And then you also should be bringing something that Marlene brings to the table, a specific something that you want to discuss might be a specific success that you've encountered and you want to teach that to other people or something that you learn here that you want to teach somebody else. So each time there's something that only Marlene's program and nobody else could have this. Well, actually, it was actually a general question because I know people drop out of masterminds in general ask to be built a relationship with the other people in the group and they feel, I don't need to pay for that anymore. So is that really a big problem or not really? It really isn't. You know, it's an interesting, it's a good question, by the way. And to my judgment, it's actually, it's not only not a big problem, but it's a good thing. I really think that if every year there's about a 20% churn in your mastermind group, it brings a lot of new life and vitality to the group. I've been a member of the Platinum Group now for this is my ninth year. And I've always enjoyed when we got when we had new members into the group in my own coaching groups. I love it when we get new people in every year. Now, you don't you don't want to have too many new people because the groups form a real bond and relationship with each other. But but about a 20% churn is very, very good for the groups. Great. And my second question is regarding that was that was two questions. This is your third question. This is a bonus question. Your bonus question is in general in the mastermind groups, what kind of problems you the number one type of problem is it more technical stuff or is it organizational stuff or marketing? Yes, all of this was the number one thing that you find over and over because I find when I'm talking to people in the group, it's it's not so much the marketing, it's organizational issues that they're dealing with. That's what comes out eventually. I still go back to my original answer. Yes, I think I actually see it's all of them. They have marketing questions, they have organization questions, time management, getting things done. Those of you in the room that are in parts of my group when you sort of agree with what what that we we just sort of go through them all and depends on the issues, which is which is a reason why, by the way, that diversity in the group is really good because, you know, and which is another reason why, by the way, I believe that, you know, if you're in a in the now this is from a different perspective, not selling it but being a member of you want to be a member of a group that's in your niche and you want to be a member of a group that's outside your niche because and that when you're selling the groups, that's that's that's one of the if it's not a niche group, that's one of the real strengths to sell it as is you have other people in there that experience other things in other industries. And but yeah, all three of the things that you asked. So like things like getting things done and time management, that's something that's not necessarily niche specific. Okay. All right, well, we I think I'm out of time. So, so I'm now going to turn it over to the Napoleon Hill. Give a break. Give a break. Yeah, give him a break. You like you like him breaks. Okay. Let's let's see how fast I can contradict whatever build did and get us both in trouble, shall we by the way, if you're speaking, you do not want to do what I did this morning. You do not want to skip the speaker in front of you unless you are really, really, really certain of what he is likely to say because countless speakers have been embarrassed by marching in having missed the speaker or speakers in front of them. And having anything happen from literally contradicting what the person prior to them said to telling the same joke. And you want to fall flat in front of an audience. Boy, that's the way to do it. And so you'll see pros. You'll see amateurs do it all the time because they're lazy. So they're out in the hall, they're up in a room, sleep in there, you know, whatever they're doing. If you watch pros. Super conference. Jim Rohn, I think was, I think he was probably in the room the whole day prior to the evening he spoke. Zigg was there from the beginning. McCann came down from the very beginning of the morning that he spoke. For the most part, it's not so much that, like Jim Rohn wanted to sit in a seminar for a day. At his age and experience level, he's probably done that enough. He wanted to make sure that there wasn't anything going on that he was going to get in trouble with or look stupid doing when he got his two and a half hours in the evening. So just as a little side tip, because there's, I find there's not much risk if you guys like modeling and copying what I do. Most of you aren't real good at that. But, but, but, you know, it occurs to me if somebody is going to do it, they're probably going to model the thing that I showed them that they shouldn't do. So just as a tip, you know, you want to, you want to at least hear the guy in front of you, if not a couple guys in front of you, unless you are very, very, very certain of what they are going to do. You see, in Bill's case, I know there's like no risk of us telling the same joke because he's not funny. But, and I'm, and I'm pretty sure what the material is going to be. But, but otherwise, you know, you want to be up here. You want to be up here first, and you want to pay attention. Also, by the way, you can usually, the flip side of that is the positive is being able to build on what somebody ahead of you has said, if you are speaking. You don't want to derail a pat presentation that you got, but you know, a sentence here, a reference here, a reference there. So if somebody has already planted certain seeds with them, you know, so the Peter Lo events, I, you know, I didn't change that presentation, but ten words in ten years. But it made a difference to me if Hopkins had been on before me or if Hopkins had not been on before me. And Tommy only did about a third of the events. I did all of them. So I didn't need to go over and hear him. I know Tommy's talk. But I needed to know if he was there or not because there were certain things then I didn't want to do. And there were certain references I wanted to make back to what Hopkins had already said, because it allowed me to build on something that had already been done by a credible authority figure that they responded to and liked. So if you're out speaking, you know, you don't want to be out in the hall, Kibbutz and, you don't want to get there two minutes before you go on. You want to pay attention to who's on the program ahead of you. And if possible, you at least want to hear the one who's on immediately before you. So you don't do anything stupid or embarrassing, and maybe you have an opportunity to do something smart. When I used to do a lot of speaking and I used to care a lot about the results, and I wasn't going to like go sit through a day if I was like an evening speaker or something. I would, and actually what reminded me of this is your reference to Fernandez. I mean, that's the 80s, right? PMA? Yeah. I was speaking at, doing a lot of speaking at PMA conferences for chiropractors, a government named Dr. Pete Fernandez, who at one time had probably the biggest, before again we were calling a coaching. Probably had the biggest coaching program anybody's ever seen with really incredible ascension and percentage above base, which we'll get back to again when we do how you get paid. Probably the funniest thing about Pete's coaching program was the ascension got to the Knights of the Fernandez roundtable thing, and Pete actually knighted them at the events with a sword. These are doctors, by the way, remember. So, you know, recognition. But so I used to do a lot of speaking at those events, and the last thing I wanted to do was go sit through, you know, the two days before I got there. So I mean, I acquired the tapes and the CDs and the products of almost everybody who was a regular on that circuit and made a point of hearing them or getting them transcribed and skimming them. So I had a sense of what everybody else was saying to them, again, for the two reasons. Number one, you don't want to look stupid. You don't want to really get in a spitting contest with somebody if you can avoid it. So, you know, if somebody is, if his main ad guy is teaching everybody white space, better I should just avoid that issue if I can, then, you know, deal with it. And I'm looking for something I can build on to make my position even better. A couple of miscellaneous items before we pick up where we left off. I wanted to say a few words more about the issue of deception, biomission, or exaggeration. And so let's take exaggeration first, because I didn't hit on it yesterday, and I just asked a question about it by somebody. So let's say you publish your schedule, as I mentioned that I did for many years. And so we would routinely, like there's a sample, I forget what page it's on, but it's almost where we left off yesterday. There's a sample, I built a sample of my schedule for you now. I don't do it much anymore, but for many years, almost every week to two weeks, we were updating that schedule and sending it out to every client and to prospects, waiting list people, etc. Ostensibly to keep them informed. So they knew, you know, what was going on and when they might be able to get to me and when they wouldn't be able to get to me and so forth. Of course, it wasn't done for that purpose at all. It was done, first of all, to reinforce the facts on the shortlist. Most important one of which is Kennedy's busier than crap, and you know, it's a miracle if you get to speak to him at midnight three Thursdays from now, and you should be happy and grateful for being squeezed in at all. So it was done to reinforce the facts on the shortlist. And it was done to stimulate business, both from clients, oh, we're going to get them to do this other thing, we better hurry up and get them now, because then otherwise we're not going to be able to get them at all, and to stimulate the prospects as well. Now, I always had two versions of the schedule. So I always had the real one, and I had the embellished one, and so a few clients saw the real one when it was actually being used for the real purpose of keeping somebody informed of exactly what was going on, and of course the internal use, staff, key associates and so forth. Now the embellished one, there was never a lie on it, but if you look at the one I gave you, which again, I don't know what page it's on right now, but you will find, for example, so let's say there's a day that you're going to hang out in the office at home in your pajamas, and you don't really know what you're going to be doing on that day, but you don't have anything booked on it, so you're going to be in the office and you're going to catch up on work and you're going to do something, you're going to be around, but who knows what you're going to do, right, because it's three Thursdays from now, and you ain't got anything booked. Well, on the public consumption schedule, I would certainly never put, day I don't know what the hell I'm going to be doing, or day I'm going to be doing nothing, nor would I even put open. I would always put office work slash copywriting for clients, or the closest I would come to indicating it was an open day would be hold, not yet scheduled, but literally almost every day on the calendar had something on it, right, and the busier it looks, the better. So that's not a lie, but it is deceptive. It indicates there's more scheduled activity than there really is. The perverse nature of consulting especially is that number one, they don't want you if you find them, they only want you if they find you. That's why overt direct marketing of yourself is the hardest thing to do, although we will talk about ways to do it before we get done here. And secondly, they pretty much only want your time if there is none of it available. If there is a bunch of it available, nobody has a particular interest in having any of it. In the non-profit world, you may or may not know this, but most non-profit organizations will show you a pie chart of where your money goes, right, and X percentage goes to this, and X percentage goes to that, and X percentage goes to this, right. You may or may not know this, but the law that governs non-profits, first of all, there's nowhere near as stringent as the law that governs profits, but there is some law. The law allows them to put under education, which is always on the wheel, and it's a big section. So like if you go get the arthritis foundation, the cancer foundation, or whatever, you see their pie chart, you're going to see direct services, you're going to see a patient and consumer education, and that's going to be a big hunk of the pie chart. Administration is going to be a tiny little piece of the pie chart. It allows them to put in education any direct mail or other media that has one paragraph of copy directing people to educational resources. So therefore, virtually all of the direct mail to raise money, all that expense gets put in education. It doesn't get shown on the pie chart as money being spent to raise money. So they can spend, all they got to do is put at the bottom of an eight-page fundraising letter, one paragraph that says, you know, if you have arthritis, go to arthritis.com and access these resources. And 22 million people have arthritis, and there's four things you can do about it, and there's six home remedies that work, and they're all at the website. That now qualifies the entire expense of that mailing to be shown as education, not as fundraising. So what shows up as fundraising on the chart is only the things that you can't legally show anyplace else in order to minimize, because if you show people exactly what was going on, so 72 cents out every dollar is being spent to get another dollar, and only 28 cents is left, and 14 cents of that's going to administration. See, if you reconstituted the pie chart is what it really is, and you looked at it, you'd think twice about continuing to give money to X, Y, Z foundation because administration's like 50% of the money that's left over after the money's spent getting more money. So they're allowed to show it, so the only thing that's under fundraising is like the expense for an event, so anything that can be moved over in education, so if they produce a DVD, that's all about raising money. So let's say it's about getting you to leave money to them in your will. All they got to have on a DVD is 10% of the minutes have to be direct education about how you can help whatever the condition is that there is money for, and the entire expense of the DVD moves over into education on the pie chart, it doesn't have to be shown as fundraising or advertising or marketing. It allows them to show a much better pie chart. And that it's deceptive, it's not a lie, and it's not illegal, but the perception would be very different of people giving money to these organizations if we reconstituted the pie chart and didn't have the liberty to do that. Pretty much everybody in the nonprofit world is okay with all of that. In political fundraising, a lot of people, their attitudes would probably be a lot different if they knew two things about giving money to candidates. One is that when the candidate quits, whatever money is left in his campaign account that he has to spend, he gets to keep, tax-free. So everybody was sort of annoyed in the last presidential election, those who supported him, that Gore actually finished up and didn't spend all the money. Well, that lays there now in case he decides to do something again, but eventually, if he decides to do nothing, tits. And so senators, if they end in a good incumbent who nobody serious runs against them, and they don't have to spend a lot of money getting reelected, but they keep raising a lot of money, when it's all said and done, they may have six, seven, eight million dollars unspent in their campaign account. Well, that goes in their pocket. So you may or may not like that when you support a candidate. It's not that they ever, nobody ever lies about it, but they don't tell you. I mean, so is it deceptive by omission? You know, if you put that as a disclosure statement on every request for money, the other thing, there is no law about second-party dealing. By that I mean my campaign for the U.S. Senate can get all its printing done by a print shop that my spouse owns. Thereby, the profit margin on the printing is going into our personal account. And we're making money on all the money that's being spent running the campaign. Now, there's nothing illegal about it. There's no law against it other than that the charges have to be reasonable and customary. So the print shop can't charge $50 for something that should cost five, but they can charge reasonable and customary fees with reasonable profits in it. So if the candidate gets a self-published book done and his brother-in-law owns the book printing company, as long as the charge is reasonable and customary, the profit goes to the brother-in-law, who he had to support anyway, so better he does it with public dollars rather than private dollars, right? So now, if that was all disclosed, would people feel differently about, yeah, but it doesn't have to be disclosed. So it's deception by omission, by non-statement. Everybody draws their lines about all this stuff situationally. Everybody's got a moving line. Anybody in here that says they got a permanent line or anybody who tells you they got a permanent line, they don't. You make these kind of decisions as you go. And we all do, right, make these kind of decisions as we go, right? So Bill tells you they decided to sell the CDs last night and printed up the order form in the dead of night because everybody would want it, right? Now that happens probably to be absolutely true. There was probably no advance thought to this, but would it matter if there was? Well, you got to decide that for yourself from both sides of that equation. Would it matter if that was a gambit? Would it matter? It doesn't really. It might matter in effectiveness, but does it matter to the end user who's getting the CDs? It's like a tele-seminar that is replayed and pretended to be live, and the people believe it is live because they more show up that way. But it's not live. So is that deceptive? Obviously it's deceptive. Now it can be blatantly deceptive. It can be deceptive by implication and assumption. It can be deceptive by omission, but it's deceptive. What's the end result? Well, the person on the line has the exact same experience they would have as if the tele-seminar was live. If they think it's live and it sounds live and it behaves live and people get to ask questions and they're pushing the star button and they just don't get through, okay? They have entirely the same experience as if you did it live. So is there any harm? No. If they buy from it, were they cheated or defrauded or any of that in some way? No. Because if they had bought from it live, the end result's the same. They pay the same price. They get the same stuff. They get the same value out of it, whatever value it is that they go get out of it. But in theory, the only truly non-deceptive thing to do would be to fully disclose this is not a live tele-... somebody come on at the beginning. This is not a live tele-seminar. Everybody should know this is a pre-recorded tele-seminar. It was pre-recorded 322 years ago. The guy's dead. You know, et cetera. And obviously, you could hear the hang-ups all across the country. Click, click, click, click, click, click, click, click, click. Because everybody left, right? So, you know, just a little bit about that. The second thing I would say is that it is important to understand everything that is talked about here has to be applied differently in different situations. And you have to give some careful thought and not just run off bull in China shop, especially if you got something that's working and just change it all because you sat in a seminar for two days and you heard an example or you really gotta think these things through. For example, there's a difference between local versus national. There's a difference between niche versus non-niche. In the case of the IBAs who are in the room who are running local chapters, that's really sort of a catcher, a point of entry device. And your behavior as a chapter president and running local feel good meetings has to be a little different than your behavior running the coaching meeting where people ascend and are paying serious money and are behind serious doors. So this is just sort of a basis for thought about now what should I do in my situation with my people, with my circumstances. It is not like Moses coming down the hill with the 11 commandments and everybody is supposed to follow them literally. Everything has to be considered in the context of your particular market, your particular clientele, your particular fee structure and so forth. With all of that said, let's sort of pick up where we left off in the getting of clients, the attraction of clients. And we were right after molehills and the mountains. And so if you flip over to your page 55, that's approximately where we were. And we're gonna go all the way through and finish getting clients, we're gonna talk about getting paid, financial compensation structures. And then we're gonna go through all the rest of the documents and the samples and so forth of things that I have to show you. If you, so if you're on page 55, Keith Oberman on MSNBC, the countdown show starts every night with the 10 stories that they're gonna talk about coming up. Click, click, click, click, click, click, click. And the voiceover is, which of these stories will you be talking about tomorrow? It's a very good question. Which part of you are people gonna be talking about tomorrow? Because if you are not being talked about continuously, good or bad, by the way, better if it's more good than bad, but if you are not being talked about continuously by your clientele amongst themselves and to others. For example, again, we watched the Muse last night. And so think back to the beginning of that segment where what's that actor's name is telling the comedian Jeff, yeah, very good, I'm very underrated actor. So Jeff Bridges is now telling the other guy about her, right? Well, that's exactly the way you would want somebody, telling somebody about you. That won't happen by accident. You know, that has to sort of be planted. But if she's got like 10 people telling that story to somebody that week, she has no marketing problems. She has no client getting problems, but you gotta be continuously talked about amongst your clientele. You gotta be the topic of interest and speculation and curiosity and blabbering, and you gotta be talked about in your market. I never go to them. Occasionally somebody will download something and send it to me, but there are like chat rooms where people go and talk about Dan Kennedy. On one level it's sort of sad and pathetic. It's sort of in the get a life category. But for example, Paris Hilton, who of course makes all her money by being Paris Hilton. And no one should miss the lesson of that. The current going rate, for example, to have Paris Hilton at your party for two hours is $125,000. She is sometimes doing two a night. I'm sorry. See now where I was gonna go is you can get in and a call for less money now that she's dead. But say both your remark and mine would be better off not said. But anyway, think about this. She is getting paid large sums of money for who she is. There are at last count over 300 different websites where people go to talk about Paris Hilton. Some of them are very negative and critical and nasty. The site's about the 10 worst celebrities in Hollywood and their behavior this week. Others are fan club type sites and everything in between. But her elevation to a form of stardom and certainly to being able to make a lot of money by being Paris Hilton is all about being talked about. The lunatic kid, what's her name? Brittany, in case you didn't notice. She's been in the news for the last three days nonstop because she did her one day in rehab. Which is an all time record. But it suggests a business. Rehab in 24 hours or less. We bring you in, we fix you, we get your ass out because you gotta go make a movie. And I bet you could charge more for that than six months worth of rehab and you get them in and out in the day. You only gotta feed them three times. But she has shaved her head and gotten tattoos all over her head. Not about the long term strategy of all that. But she's the only thing being talked about on CNN. I mean, when I clicked on CNN last night, ahead of the story of the North Korean nuke negotiations which might actually like affect us. I mean, look, I remember being under the wood desk doing the drill, which you know, Louis Black's routine about that is great. That's the first time you realize all adults are morons when you're a kid, right? Is when they are putting you under a wood desk to protect you from a large flaming orb object that is gonna descend upon the building. But I mean, ahead of the North Korean nuke story is Brittany shaving her head and getting tattoos. And what's everybody talking about? Brittany shaving her head and getting tattoos. What does that do? Drives more people to all the Brittany Talk websites, sales, Brittany CD, sales, Brittany records, sales, Brittany t-shirts. So being talked about is a very important thing. And it is a big part of this business. And forgive me about names, but the gentleman was just up here asking questions who with the funny accent. Where are you? There you are. Yeah, he was talking to me on the break and he's become the celebrated real estate guru in Australia with a 50% mix of really good press and really, really, really bad press. The evil predatory guy bringing these rotten, sneaky tactics from the United States and stealing people's property for no money and teaching people how to be pirates and on and on and on and on and on, right? And yeah, I remember our rule, speak when spoken to, remember? You gotta keep him in a little, he's like Tyson, you gotta keep him in a little box, let him out every once in a while, do something, put him back in a box. We had a client years ago who 60 minutes did the just this huge hatchet job on. I mean, it was just a massacre because of course the fool thought he could handle them. And so he spent all week with Mike Walls. Oh, they taped thousands of hours of crap which they then edited down into 20 minutes of slaughter. And I mean, and he's in a niche, so I mean, it was ugly. I mean, the day afterwards, it was pre-internet so it didn't spread as fast. You couldn't jump on and everybody emailing everybody. But still, the phone lines are buzzing. He's in a niche and maybe at that time 35,000 people. So I mean, literally, if everybody talks to three people, pretty soon everybody's talking about it, right? And I mean, the clients are calling and so, there was about a week of real true ugliness, no appreciable losses that you could directly track to it, real true ugliness. By about a month later, the reaction is, oh yeah, we saw him on 60 Minutes. Oh, we saw you on 60 Minutes. Two months later, we're using it in the ads as seen on 60 Minutes. Now we're probably the only people in the world that ever got a cease and desist letter from 60 Minutes for using ads seen on 60 Minutes in our advertising. But nobody remembered anything except he's on TV, fabulous. They know the show, they know TV, they know him as a celebrity, right? We're using it. So, I mean, better you should be talked about in a positive way, but better to be talked about than not talked about and you try and stimulate it and control it as best you can. Some of the ways that you can make sure that you are talked about are on this list. You can, and this is especially appropriate to those of you who work in niches, but you can attack and criticize the status quo. There is always some status quo that everybody pretty much thinks are a bunch of idiots or are evil or are somehow their enemy. With salespeople, it's the home office, right? So as long as you're not getting your money from the home office, you can constantly attack the home office. So all of the idiots in the ivory tower back at corporate headquarters who were spending all their time going to lunch and dreaming up stupid crap for you to do, see sales that resonates with salespeople, all right? A lot of people don't remember, but J. Abraham used to run this campaign niche by niche by niche. It was a very good campaign. I swiped it and applied it in different ways. And it was, who is this man and why is he saying these terrible things about life insurance companies? And he would run that in a life insurance trade journal. Who is this man and why is he saying these terrible things about real estate? And he would run that in a real estate and then he would make fun of the corporations and how stupid their advertising was and how stupid their marketing was and how the agents were suffering because they had to put up with all these morons and that resonates. So you can attack and criticize the status quo. I, God knows how many millions of dollars directly and indirectly I've taken out of the tiny little market of the National Speakers Association by spending all of my time criticizing the National Speakers Association. Now I haven't made many friends on the board from year to year to year. But it's a very popular thing to do with a segment of the association, specifically the people I want out of it, which are those who think like business people and are already frustrated. So you don't have to be around there very long and go into meetings and stuff if you're a business person before you realize that 70% of the folks are clueless. And now you're frustrated, just like Buster Kravitz said, well, I attract those people like a magnet by criticizing the entire culture of the association. And so for 20 years, I've made fun of their certifications and their award thing. And it's easy because the thing is ridiculous. But simply by banging away at that, I'm talked about. I'm reviled by some. And I magnetically attract others. And I always made a point when I was working the niche heavily, I always made a point, for example, to do some kind of major mailing about four to five to six weeks before the National Convention, in which I made all sorts of annoying and critical and provocative statements. And then I show up at the convention and make sure I'm walking around very visible. I usually went and worked during the sessions and then came down on the breaks. And it's the opposite of what everybody else is doing. I mean, because what's useful to me is being accessible on the break and just let them find me. And here they come. Boy, you hit it right on the nail. And they're all business people. And they're exactly who I want. So you can attack and criticize status quo and attack and criticize popular enemies. You can be a crusader. Gore has reinvented himself very effectively to give credit where credit is due. He's gone from irrelevant to very relevant in a very short period of time by essentially turning us back on the traditional political channels and circles and all of that and picking an issue and crusading like the Ralph Nader of old and appealing to the liberal segment of the entertainment community. And we're going to see him get an Oscar for gone conclusion. The odds, by the way, you've got to give two to get one. It's one to two the last time I checked where you can bet it legally in London. And I bet it. So you'll know if I did well. But so I got a little money on it at one to one, and I got a little money on it at one to two. By little, I mean little to me, not necessarily little to you. I think it's a pretty good investment. But you do know the way you can bet on anything legally in London. I mean, they got odds on everything. So from day to day, current events stuff you can bet. You can bet who's going to be ahead in the presidential poll next week. You can bet that now if you want to, by how many points are going to be ahead. So if you like to entertain yourself, you can do that. You can get yourself attacked. See, a lot of people think that's bad, but it's not bad. Not necessarily bad. It's OK to be attacked by somebody that calls attention to you, to the people you want, who will like the fact that you're being attacked, and will respond to you being attacked. Or in a big public way, think about Atkins. 30 years, I don't think Dr. Atkins got one piece of favorable PR anywhere. He made his entire living and built his entire company by freely going any place he was invited. Talk shows, university lectures, medical conventions, and letting them all attack him. And Atkins got bigger and bigger and bigger and bigger. And the more the medical establishment attacked it, the bigger it got. Why? Because there's a bigger segment in the American population that doesn't like the medical establishment than there is that likes the medical establishment. So there's more people who think MDs are wrong than trust MDs to be right. So if you're going to get attacked by somebody, get attacked by the MDs. And Atkins was very good at it. I mean, he just sat there and took punches for 15 or 20 minutes and said, well, yeah. But look, Mary lost 238 pounds. Billy Bob lost 148 pounds. She lost this kind of pounds. How can you argue with that? And people went and bought Atkins stuff. And the brand got bigger and bigger and bigger and bigger. So you can be attacked. You can have a scandal to talk about. Larry King, who was on the Peter Low events all the time, has to say, Larry, there's got to be a slow news day sometime. When nothing's happening and you've got no guest, I'm available. Larry would say, easy. You can be on a show tomorrow. Just kill your parents. Well, think about it. So somebody's involved in a scandal. Usually they temporarily look bad. If they're handled well, often they turn out OK. Dick Morris goes from a poorly paid employee to a highly paid pundit and author. People have forgotten all about the toe-sucking prostitute thing, letting her listen to the conversations with Clinton on the phone. That's all gone. But it made him famous. Then he went away and did political consulting for a year in Australia, I think. And then he came back and he's big. At a certain level, scandal fascinates people. That horrible Mel Gibson movie that otherwise nobody would have gone and seen, with the Amazon, whatever that thing is, actually did better after his arrest for his anti-Semitic remarks than it could possibly have done without all of that PR. The best one is the Trump-Rosie deal, both benefit. That's like, I doubt they cooked it up, because I think they genuinely dislike each other. But if they had cooked it up, it's brilliant. I mean, brilliant. And he starts it a week before the new season of the apprentice. The whole week before the new season of the apprentice, that's all everybody's talking about. There's the two of them fighting with each other. And where did he get most of the coverage? On a rival network who would never have promoted him or the apprentice, ABC. They ain't going to put him on their shows the week before the apprentice is premiering. But Good Morning America had him on to ask him about the Rosie Feud. Every day on The View, she's talking about him. He was on ABC World News, Ask About the Feud. So he's on ABC everything. He was on Jimmy Kimmel. He was everywhere. He wouldn't have got on those shows without that feud. So there's lots of ways to get yourself talked about. And the most important thing about it is it needs to be continuing. If they are not talking about you, what you ought to realize is they're talking about somebody else. And this even goes to Marlene's question. It's a broader answer to your narrow question. I believe either the second or the third of your six questions when you were up here. Hey, there's a lesson to be learned from her. She ain't shy. She'll take what she can get. Pushy is good, especially for women. Pushy broads do better than non-pushy broads. Part of the answer to your question is what about them leaving mastermind groups and splintering off and forming their own groups and thinking they don't need you? And Bill's answer was a pragmatic answer, which is a correct answer and a correctly pragmatic answer. It's make sure that parts of what happens in every program you do are connected to you, directly linked to you, are about you, are being provided by you. So there's some portion that obviously can't be replicated somewhere else. And there are people who have this problem. An information marketer I won't mention has had four in the past two years, otherwise very successful information market. But it's had four different people go splinter and take people out of his group and form their own group. And why should we pay for this now? We can all get together for free kind of thing. And they can. They can all get together for free except not with him. And now over a period of time, they're coming back. They're leaving the doofus who led them off into the winter land and they're coming back. But the bigger, broader answer to this, layered over top of the pragmatic answer that Bill gave you, is that, again, keeping them is about a lot more than the core values of what they're getting from the thing by being in a group. Keeping them is as much about their ongoing interest and fascination in you in general. And so if it's you, they're all talking about. And they leave you. Now what are they going to talk about? So you've got to have them perpetually interested in you and what you're up to and what you're thinking and what your opinions are. And what is she going to say next? And all of that. And then they won't go away. Even if the thought occurs to them, they won't go away. OK, here's some examples of strategies to be talked about. Number one, do out of ordinary things. Vacationing in China is much more interesting than vacationing in Dubuque. Now Matt happens to have other reasons to go to China. But since he has reasons to go to China, he is able to use it and uses it to the hilt because most people don't go to China. So that immediately is a very interesting thing. And so every year for two weeks, well, Matt's in China. What everybody's talking about is Matt's in China. What's he doing over there? What's he going to bring back? Everybody's emailing each other. Matt's in China. I don't talk to him much. I don't work with him directly. I obviously don't get an email. I somehow know when he's in China. Why? Because people are telling me. Matt's in China again for two whole weeks. I mean, now if he was in LA, so what? Yannick has this adventurer thing. He's forked over the money to be one of the first 100 that Richard Branson shoots up in space in a rocket. I don't know when that's going to happen, but it's not next week. So it's relatively cheap money because you're probably going to get to talk about it for two or three years before it actually happens. In platinum, we're all going to kick in 500 bucks a piece and buy a life insurance policy. Well, I mean, if he's got to go, he's got to go. I told him, see, my idea was they do that for his next boot camp. The bonus with your early bird registration is you get 1 tenth of the life insurance policy he takes out on himself when they blast him off into space. So everybody can come and watch the launch. And some will hope one way. Some will hope the other way. But whatever the outcome, it's good. He didn't care for the recommendation, but I thought it was clever. You know, take a chance. But I think that's $175,000. I think that's what that is. 200. OK, well, I think he got him down to 175. So there's room to negotiate. I'm pretty sure he's paying 175 between you and I. But either way. Now, one thing to know about that is a lot of people immediately blanch at 175, just like at 75 to hire George Foreman or whatever. But you've got to think long and hard about those kind of numbers. And I'm serious. In this case, he's probably got a two or three year run up before this happens. So really, let's say it was 150 so I can do easy math. And it's three years before he shoots. So really, he's paying $50,000 a year for this thing. And there are going to be some Richard Branson parties and crap that they get to go to along the way. I mean, there's supports to all this. So there's some events. And there's pictures with Branson. And there's pictures of the rocket being built and all that. This is probably really cheap money. Because this is a way to get talked about in his universe again and again and again and again. Horse racing, for me, is more useful than if I played golf. Especially the way I play golf. But last time I played golf, I knocked myself unconscious. No, no, no. That's true story. That's true story. I mean, I got a really bad slice and no loft. So when I drive the balls this far off the ground, psssh. But not like that. It's more like, meh, over there. So I'm no good at this. So I mean, oh, and my drives go about three feet. I can putt like crazy, but I need a designated driver. So I'm not on this golf course. And I am absolutely determined to send this ball somewhere. So I mean, I wind up like Babe Ruth. And I mean, I hit this thing with everything I got. The next thing I know, I'm looking up at paramedics. The ball goes this far off the ground at an angle into one of them little metal signs that say golf cart crossing. And it hits the metal sign. And it comes back off the metal sign, 900 miles an hour, right into my head. Boom. I'm about like a rock. I wake up looking at the paramedics. I said, jeez, Doc, what do you think? He says, I think you got the worst slice I've ever seen in my entire life. So horse racing is more useful to be the playing golf, because lots of people play golf, hardly anybody, since it's an assault game and races horses. It's more useful. Now, it so happens that's what I want to do anyway. But since I do it, I want to use it, right? The other thing is an aside, by the way, your extraordinary activities. See, so Yannick's thing. Well, Yannick wants to do that anyway. So it's something he really wants to do. But now it's tax deductible. Just to do it for fun, it isn't. But see, the 175s really only cost him 120, something like that, because he's in the top tax bracket. So there's a little side financial benefit to this, too. So do out of ordinary things. And anytime that you do, make a big deal out of them. You know, the photograph of Beyond the Bowl. And I think it cost me, when I headed down, it cost me like $5,000 between the money to rent the bowl and the money for the photographer and the drugs for the bowl. Courageous I are not. That picture has probably, it's a million dollar photograph. Easy, maybe more. Because in part, it's a weird and strange thing. I mean, of the 10 most common questions I get, probably one of them is always about the bowl. From strangers, from clients, from people, LA seminar. We had a lot of first timers at the LA seminar in December of last year. A lot of new people. Questions about the bowl from everybody. Do you own the bowl? Is the bowl in your backyard? What's the bowl's name? What kind of bowl is that? Big white. You know? What does that bowl eat anyway? How did you get them to stand still for the picture? On and on and on and on and on. It would be better if they used their time and their opportunity to ask me a useful question. But from my standpoint, the bowl thing was brilliant. Ordinary picture, standing next to Mercedes, standing next to airplane. Lots of people do that. How many people got a picture of themselves sitting on a bowl? Not too many. And I ripped it right off from a car dealer, by the way. That's where I got the idea. There's a guy in Phoenix, Tex Earnhardt, who owns car dealerships for years and sits on a bowl. And he copied Cal Worthington. If you go far enough back to no Cal Worthington. So Tex Earnhardt signed off and all his TV commercials was. And that ain't no bowl. And so in Phoenix, you saw it like 6 million times a day. And it was implanted in my subconscious. But it's been a million dollar picture because it's unusual. So do out of ordinary things. Make known your eccentricities. Back to the list of facts. One of the things people know about Sam Walton. And it was more important, obviously, a few years ago than it is now, since Sam's gone. But Sam's never left. Still lived in Bentonville, Arkansas. Drove his old pickup truck. Went down, ate at the diner, breakfast with the boys, just like he always had. No matter how much money he wound up with. Well, that's odd. It's also only partly true. I mean, yes, he had the old pickup truck. And occasionally he made sure he was seeing driving it. He had about 20 other vehicles, too, that weren't old pickup trucks. And occasionally he went and had breakfast with the boys. But it wasn't like he was daily down at the diner in the pickup truck. But that's like what people had in their heads. And that's what they talked about. When they talked about Sam Walton, they talked about that. It was useful to him. Warren Buffett. Well, Warren Buffett is amongst the things he's known for, he may be best known for his refusal to invest in any tech stocks. Well, that's an eccentricity, especially given his connection to Gates. But it works because we know it about him. And because it is rather strange. Be associated with famous or fascinating people, we've talked about that. And we will come back and talk about it. Pick a public fight. We've talked about that. Trump learned a lesson from the Rosie thing. He's now, like she's picking one a week, so is he. He's figured out this strategy works a lot. So he's picked a big fight with the city of Palm Beach, Florida, over his flag, which is a really good thing to pick a fight over, by the way. You see him last night on World Wrestling? No, it was your World Wrestling? No. You're taking a fight, one of them's going to shave your head supposedly. Oh, OK. With the head guy there. Oh, with McMahon? Now, there's another useful thing about you mentioning that. So what he mentioned is Trump was on World Wrestling last night, and he's picked a fight with Vince McMahon, the guy that owns WWF, and what I'm going to shave their head, or something that's a result of all that. Well, if you don't watch World Wrestling, none of us saw this, right? And you might think, what the hell's the benefit to him with the wrestling? Well, you would underestimate the size of the wrestling family based number one. You would underestimate its demographics, because it is not on a bunch of day-to-day people who live in trailer parks anymore. It once was that, just like NASCAR once was that. But it's not that anymore. By the way, you know what the fastest-growing segment of NASCAR fan base is? Hmm? Hmm? Hmm? Hmm? Hmm? Hmm? Hmm? Dave McMahon. Somewhat to the consternation of the original fan base. But the fastest-growing segment of their fan base is Dave McMahon. Second fastest is women. You're right. So he's absolutely right to be there, because he's reaching an audience that includes some of the people that would normally be in his audience, but also includes a whole bunch of people who might normally not pay attention to him. And Trump's money today is not very far afield from Paris Hilton's money. And the lesson there is, is our money's not very far afield from that either. Trump's money is tied to being Trump, not to real estate development. That was a decade ago. Now, Trump's money is from being Trump. And the more Trump is, the more money Trump gets. Because they give him points to stick his name on a project, for example. Front-end points. And everybody might usually get five points. He gets eight points. Then he gets more famous. He gets 10 points. Then he gets more famous. And he gets 12 points. Well, an extra point. Big deal in a 500-unit high-rise condo project in the Bahamas. That's a big deal. It's all about being Trump. So he's out there humping every place he can be. A lot of places we don't see him unless you watch that thing. Say, I don't watch wrestling. So I would, right? You were out, but you hit it by accident. But millions watch it. And now some of them who paid no attention to him before are going to start paying attention to him. And now they're going to go watch the apprentice. And then some of them, when there's a seminar ad, they're going to go to the learning annex seminar when they wouldn't have gone before, on and on and on and on and on. So pick a public fight. But pick a good one. Perform amazing feats. The guy who was really good at this, he's pretty much retired now. And a lot of people have copied him cleverly as Kreskin. And so Kreskin, who made money by being Kreskin, speaker, entertainer, et cetera, the amazing Kreskin, how many remember? OK. So Kreskin like a mentalist. What would you call him, Dave? A mentalist. And Kreskin's amazing feat, which he did at least twice a year on Regis for as long. I mean, when Regis was on in LA, he was on Regis and Kathy, and Regis and Donna, and Regis and whoever is sitting next to Regis at the moment. Kreskin's amazing feat is come on the show, and they've hidden an envelope somewhere in Manhattan. And they go find it. Over and over and over again. So Kreskin had this routine, that you saw him do something amazing. You got Blaine now, who is really sort of following that model in their field, if you will. Paul Hartunian, most of you have seen Paul's stuff. Well, what's Paul's like, claim to fame? Whenever I write a copy about Paul, what do I use first and foremost? Exactly. This is the guy who really sold the Brooklyn Bridge. Because that's like the joke, sell the Brooklyn Bridge. He really sold the Brooklyn Bridge, which he did. He went and got a bunch of wood, cut it up, little pieces that came from the bridge and sold it. So we can say he really sold the Brooklyn Bridge. Well, that was an amazing feat. Now, I don't know if, I think when he did it, he did it like actually to make the money selling the wood. And wasn't thinking much further than that. But it ain't really a hard feat to replicate. Somebody's knocking down something somewhere every day. So Robert Allen, the real estate guy, for many years, Robert Allen lived off of the challenge. And he did two of them for publicity. One is, you can drop me in any city in America, anywhere, with $500 in my pocket, no contacts, no nothing, turned me loose. And 48 hours later, I will have bought a piece of real estate. And occasionally, the media would take him up on it, and he would do it. The other was, I'll take anybody off the soup line, a homeless person, anybody. And within 72 hours, they will have bought a house. And they would do that. And he would get massive PR from it, and then use it in his advertising. So that's an amazing feat.
